skill.name: schema-research
skill.description: Research Schema.org classes and properties for Logseq Template Graph projects. It checks official vocabulary, maps types to Logseq properties, and suggests integration steps.
skill.summary: Research Schema.org vocabulary and map it into Logseq template classes and properties.
skill.author: C0ntr0lledCha0s

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many command and weak-crypto patterns, but review found most command hits are fenced documentation examples and no cryptographic code was found. The confirmed risks are legitimate network lookups to Schema.org and helper scripts that launch a browser with a user-provided Schema.org path. No evidence found of credential access, data exfiltration, malware behavior, or prompt injection attempts.

medium_findings.title: Helper Scripts Launch External Browser Process
medium_findings.description: The shell and PowerShell helper scripts build a Schema.org URL from user input and open it through local browser launch commands. The host is fixed to schema.org and shell arguments are quoted in the Bash script, but the scripts still perform local process launch and outbound browsing behavior.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: Direct script evidence shows URL construction from user input followed by xdg-open, open, start, or Start-Process. The behavior is legitimate for a lookup helper, but it is still executable network-facing behavior.

low_findings.title: External Schema.org Network Lookups
low_findings.description: The skill instructs the assistant to fetch Schema.org pages and includes Schema.org URLs in examples and helper scripts. This is expected for a Schema.org research skill, but users should know that requested class or property names may be sent to Schema.org.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines explicitly reference WebFetch and construct https://schema.org URLs. The destination is the official Schema.org domain and no credential or secret transmission was found.

low_findings.title: Static Command Findings Are Mostly Documentation Examples
low_findings.description: Many external command detections occur in fenced examples that show grep, ls, npm, and git commands for local project research. They are not hidden execution logic, but users should review commands before running examples from community skills.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited commands are inside Markdown code fences or tool examples and are presented as user workflow guidance. No automatic execution path was found in these documentation sections.

low_findings.title: No Confirmed Weak Cryptography Despite Static Hits
low_findings.description: Static analysis flagged weak cryptographic algorithm patterns, but review found Schema.org and Logseq vocabulary examples rather than hashing, encryption, or password handling code. No evidence found of MD5, SHA1, credential storage, or custom cryptographic implementation.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Targeted review of the reported files found documentation and vocabulary content, not cryptographic operations. Confidence is high, but this finding records a dismissal of broad static matches rather than a positive vulnerability.

dangerous_patterns.title: Browser Opener Commands
dangerous_patterns.description: The Bash helper invokes xdg-open, open, or start with a generated Schema.org URL, and the PowerShell helper invokes Start-Process.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The process launch commands are directly visible in executable script files. The risk is moderated because they open a fixed-domain URL rather than arbitrary shell text.

dangerous_patterns.title: Network Fetch Instructions
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill directs assistants to fetch current Schema.org pages for class, property, and hierarchy research.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The WebFetch instructions and Schema.org URLs are explicit. This is expected skill functionality, but it requires network access disclosure.

content.value_statement: Choosing Schema.org classes and properties for a Logseq template is slow and error-prone. This skill researches official vocabulary, maps types, and turns findings into integration guidance.
actual_capabilities: Fetches and summarizes official Schema.org class and property pages.
Traces class inheritance from Thing to the target class.
Maps Schema.org types to Logseq property types and cardinality choices.
Checks whether classes or properties already exist in a template project.
Suggests module placement, related classes, and next integration steps.
Provides example research reports for classes and properties.
limitations: Requires network access when fetching current Schema.org documentation.
Does not automatically modify Logseq template files unless separately instructed.
Helper scripts only open Schema.org pages and do not validate vocabulary correctness.
Recommendations depend on the current template structure available in the workspace.

use_cases.title: Plan a New Template Class
use_cases.description: Research a Schema.org class before adding it to a Logseq Template Graph module.

use_cases.title: Audit Missing Properties
use_cases.description: Compare an existing class against Schema.org and prioritize missing properties.

use_cases.title: Validate Type Mapping
use_cases.description: Choose Logseq property types and cardinality values from Schema.org expected types.

prompt_templates.title: Research a Class
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner lookup for one Schema.org class.
prompt_templates.prompt: Research the Schema.org Recipe class for my Logseq Template Graph. Show its hierarchy, key properties, and suggested module placement.

prompt_templates.title: Compare Existing Properties
prompt_templates.scenario: Find gaps in a current template class.
prompt_templates.prompt: Compare my existing Person class with Schema.org Person. List missing high-value properties and explain which ones to add first.

prompt_templates.title: Validate a Property Design
prompt_templates.prompt: Validate the Schema.org birthDate property for Logseq. Recommend the property type, cardinality, and example values.

prompt_templates.title: Create an Integration Plan
prompt_templates.prompt: Research Event, Place, and Organization relationships in Schema.org. Propose a Logseq integration plan with reused properties and module boundaries.

output_examples.input: Research the Recipe class before I add it.
output: A class report that identifies Recipe as a CreativeWork subclass.
A prioritized property list covering ingredients, instructions, timing, yield, cuisine, and diet information.
A recommendation to place the class in the creative-work module with related nutrition and instruction classes.

output_examples.input: What properties are missing for Person?
output: A comparison between current template properties and official Schema.org Person properties.
A ranked list of missing fields such as givenName, familyName, contactPoint, and affiliation.
Guidance on type mapping, cardinality, and whether each property belongs in a common or person module.

output_examples.input: Should location be a node or text field?
output: A type recommendation based on Schema.org expected values.
Examples showing when Place should become a linked node and when plain text is acceptable.
A reuse check for existing location properties across event, organization, and place modules.

best_practices: Fetch current Schema.org documentation before recommending a new class or property.
Reuse existing template properties before creating new module-specific ones.
Document inherited properties separately from direct class properties.
anti_patterns: Do not duplicate inherited Schema.org properties in every child class.
Do not add custom names when an official Schema.org property already fits.
Do not run helper scripts with sensitive or private query terms.

faq.question: Does this skill change my Logseq files automatically?
faq.answer: No. It provides research and integration guidance. File edits require a separate instruction.

faq.question: Why does it use network access?
faq.answer: It fetches current Schema.org pages so recommendations match the latest vocabulary.

faq.question: Can it research both classes and properties?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes workflows for class hierarchy research and property type validation.

faq.question: Does it support Claude, Codex, and Claude Code?
faq.answer: Yes. The report lists support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.

faq.question: Are the included scripts required?
faq.answer: No. They are optional helpers that open Schema.org pages in a browser.

faq.question: What should I review before publishing?
faq.answer: Review the network lookup behavior and browser launch scripts, especially for community marketplace use.
